perturbation.original_text: 3.3 Benefits. The Consultant shall not be entitled to any benefits, coverages or privileges, including, without limitation, health insurance, social security, unemployment, medical or pension payments, made available to employees of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 Benefits. The Consultant shall not be entitled to any benefits, coverages or privileges, including, without limitation, medical or pension payments, made available to employees of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit exclusion of 'social security' and 'unemployment' benefits, the contract becomes ambiguous regarding the consultant's entitlement to these benefits. This ambiguity could potentially violate Massachusetts law if the consultant is misclassified as an independent contractor when they should be classified as an employee, thus entitling them to unemployment and social security benefits.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 151A, § 2 details the requirements for unemployment benefits. By omitting the explicit exclusion of 'unemployment' and given certain factual situations (e.g., the consultant being de facto an employee), this section may be violated if the consultant should have been classified as an employee and thus entitled to unemployment benefits, but the company denies them based on the contract's ambiguity.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 151A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 151A UNEMPLOYMENT INSURANCE Section 2 Service deemed ''employment''; exclusions Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Service deemed ''employment''; exclusions Section 2. Service performed by an individual, except in such cases as the context of this chapter otherwise requires, shall be deemed to be employment subject to this chapter irrespective of whether the common-law relationship of master and servant exists, unless and until it is shown to the satisfaction of the commissioner that— (a) such individual has been and will continue to be free from control and direction in connection with the performance of such services, both under his contract for the performance of service and in fact; and (b) such service is performed either outside the usual course of the business for which the service is performed or is performed outside of all the places of business of the enterprise for which the service is performed; and (c) such individual is customarily engaged in an independently established trade, occupation, profession or business of the same nature as that involved in the service performed. The failure to withhold federal or state income taxes or to pay workers compensation premiums with respect to an individual's wages shall not be used for the purposes of making a determination under this section. An individual's exercise of the option to purchase insurance as permitted by subsection (4) of section 1 of chapter 152 shall not be used for purposes of making a determination under this section. Whoever fails to treat an individual as an employee according to this chapter shall be punished as provided in section 47. Nothing in this section shall limit the availability of other remedies at law or in equity. × Register for MyLegi

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit exclusion of 'social security' and 'unemployment' benefits, creating ambiguity about the consultant's entitlement to these benefits. This omission contradicts Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 151A, Section 2, which defines 'employment' and outlines the criteria under which service is deemed employment subject to unemployment insurance. Specifically, the law states that service performed by an individual is considered employment unless certain conditions regarding control, location of service, and independent trade are met. The omission in the contract could lead to a misclassification of the consultant as an independent contractor when they should be classified as an employee, potentially violating their entitlement to unemployment benefits under this section. The original text explicitly excluded these benefits, while the changed text does not, creating a scenario where the consultant's rights are not clearly defined and may be denied in contradiction to the law.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Cooperation. The Consultant shall use Consultant's best efforts in the performance of Consultant's obligations under this Agreement. The Company shall provide such access to its information and property as may be reasonably required in order to permit the Consultant to perform Consultant's obligations hereunder. The Consultant shall cooperate with the Company's personnel, shall not interfere with the conduct of the Company's business and shall observe all rules, regulations and security requirements of the Company concerning the safety of persons and property.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Cooperation. The Consultant shall use Consultant's best efforts in the performance of Consultant's obligations under this Agreement. The Company shall provide such access to its information and property as may be reasonably required in order to permit the Consultant to perform Consultant's obligations hereunder. The Consultant shall cooperate with the Company's personnel, shall not interfere with the conduct of the Company's business and shall observe all rules, regulations of the Company concerning the safety of persons and property.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the word 'security', the Consultant is no longer required to follow security requirements, and if the Company fails to take reasonable steps for safety, then the consultant may be injured. This could cause the company to violate Massachusetts workplace safety laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 149, § 6 requires employers to ensure a safe workplace. While this contract is for a consultant, if the consultant is exposed to unsafe conditions because the *security* requirement is no longer there, and the company fails to take reasonable measures for safety, the Company could be in violation of this law.

The department shall or the attorney general may investigate, from time to time, employments and places of employment, including, but not limited to, such places where temporary personnel and material elevators are used in construction, reconstruction or renovation of buildings, but excluding places of employment of persons engaged in domestic service in the home of the employer, and determine what suitable safety devices or other reasonable means or requirements for the prevention of accidents shall be adopted or followed in any or all such employments or places of employment; and also shall or the attorney general may determine what suitable devices or other reasonable means or requirements for the prevention of industrial or occupational diseases shall be adopted or followed in any or all such employments or places of employment; and shall make reasonable rules, regulations and orders for the prevention of accidents and the prevention of industrial or occupational diseases, and rules and regulations pertaining to structural painting made hereunder may provide for the payment of reasonable fees. The rules and regulations for elevators and for suitable safety devices or other reasonable means or requirements for the prevention of accidents shall also be applicable to self-employed and individual contractors who themselves work at the trade as well as to employees. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for the consultant to observe the company's security requirements. According to the law explanation, and supported by the provided scraped snippets from Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 149, Section 6, employers have a duty to ensure a safe workplace. By removing the word 'security', the contract now permits the consultant to disregard security measures, which could expose them to unsafe conditions and potentially violate M.G.L. c. 149, § 6 if the company fails to take reasonable measures for safety.

perturbation.original_text: 20. Miscellaneous. 20.1 No delay or omission by the Company in exercising any right under this Agreement shall operate as a waiver of that or any other right. A waiver or consent given by the Company on any one occasion shall be effective only in that instance and shall not be construed as a bar or waiver of any right on any other occasion.
perturbation.changed_text: 20. Miscellaneous. 20.1 No delay or omission by the Company in exercising any right under this Agreement shall operate as a waiver of that or any other right.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the second sentence of section 20.1, the contract now allows an implied waiver of rights if the company does not act on them. This runs counter to Massachusetts' contract law which requires waivers to be explicit and knowing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts law regarding waiver of contract rights
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law, as interpreted in *Commonwealth v. Consentino*, requires a clear and unequivocal waiver of rights. The omission of the explicit statement that 'A waiver or consent given by the Company on any one occasion shall be effective only in that instance and shall not be construed as a bar or waiver of any right on any other occasion' could lead a court to interpret inaction as an implied waiver, which contradicts established Massachusetts legal principles requiring waivers to be intentional and knowing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Commonwealth v. Labadie :: 2013 :: Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court Decisions :: Massachusetts Case Law :: Massachusetts Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Commonwealth v. Labadie Annotate this Case Justia Opinion Summary After a jury trial, George Labadie and Susan Carcieri, the latter of whom was employed by a federal credit union, were convicted of violating Mass. Gen. Laws. ch. 266, 52 for embezzling a “bank.” At issue on appeal was whether an employee of a federal credit union may be found guilty under section 52 of embezzlement of the credit union’s funds. The Supreme Court reversed and vacated Defendants’ convictions, holding (1) because the Commonwealth must prove under section 52 that the victim was a “bank” and because a federal credit union is not a “bank” as defined in Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 167, 1, Defendants were entitled to judgments of acquittal on this charge; (2) larceny by embezzlement is a lesser included offense of embezzlement of a bank, and federal preemption doctrine does not bar state prosecution of a federal credit union employee for larceny by embezzlement; and (3) the jury’s verdicts demonstrated that the jurors found Defendants guilty of the required elements of the lesser included offense of larceny by embezzlement. Remanded for entry of convictions of larceny by embezzlement. Read more Want to stay in the know about new opinions from the Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court? Sign up for free summaries delivered directly to your inbox. Learn More › You already receive new opinion summaries from Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court. Worcester. October 8, 2013. - February 5, 2014. Embezzlement. Credit Union. Bank. Larceny. Practice, Criminal, Lesser included offense. Jurisdiction, Superior Court, Federal preemption. Superior Court, Jurisdiction. Federal Preemption. INDICTMENTS found and
perturbation.justification: The omission of the second sentence in section 20.1 creates a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation states that the changed text now allows for an implied waiver of rights if the company does not act on them. The Law Explanation further indicates that this could lead a court to interpret inaction as an implied waiver, which contradicts established Massachusetts legal principles requiring waivers to be intentional and knowing, citing *Commonwealth v. Consentino*. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 does not directly address waivers, it does refer to Massachusetts case law (Commonwealth v. Labadie), which highlights the importance of judicial decisions in interpreting Massachusetts law. The original text explicitly prevents implied waivers, while the changed text, by omitting this provision, creates the possibility of implied waivers, directly contradicting the principle of explicit and knowing waivers required under Massachusetts law.
